data.title: Trump team to start Russia-Ukraine peace talks in Saudi Arabia without Europe: report
data.description:
Senior officials from U.S. President Donald Trump’s administration are heading to Saudi Arabia to start peace talks with Russian and Ukrainian negotiators in the coming days, Politico reported Saturday.

U.S. National Security Advisor Mike Waltz, Secretary of State Marco Rubio and Trump's Middle East envoy Steve Witkoff will travel to Saudi Arabia, the report said, citing sources familiar with the plan.

This meeting would be the first major meeting of Russian and Ukrainian representatives since war broke out in 2022.

According to the report, there are no plans for major European powers to be present at the meeting. Trump rattled allies this week by calling Russian President Vladimir Putin without consulting them or Kyiv.

General Keith Kellogg, Trump’s special envoy for Ukraine-Russia talks, said on Saturday at the Munich Security Conference in Germany that Europe won't get a seat at the table for Ukraine peace talks, Reuters reported.

Kellogg will not be present at the meeting in Saudi Arabia, Politico added.

The U.S. has reportedly sent out a questionnaire to European nations designed to gauge the continent's willingness to protect Kyiv after a settlement with Russia and scope out the price Europe is willing to pay to be involved in the talks with Moscow.

At the Munich Security Conference, European leaders have repeatedly emphasized that Ukraine must be directly involved in any peace talks between Trump and Putin.

Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskyy warned Russia was "not preparing for dialogue" to end the current war, and called for the creation of "armed forces of Europe", signaling his wavering faith in American support for the continent.

Trump has repeatedly demanded an agreement with Ukraine to access its rare earth minerals in return for the U.S. aiding the country's defense against Russia, which Ukraine has reportedly rejected.

Here are some popular oil-focused exchange-traded funds: (USO), (UCO), (DBO), (OILK), and (USL).

Here are some exchange-traded funds that track the benchmark S&P 500 index (SP500): (SPY), (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), and (SPXU).

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

More on S&P 500 Index

data.title: Apple, Amazon struggle with next-gen AI upgrades for voice assistants
data.description:
Apple's (NASDAQ:AAPL) overhaul for its digital assistant Siri is facing engineering problems and software bugs, Bloomberg reported Friday, which might delay or limit the release of the long-awaited upgrade.

The iPhone maker first unveiled an AI-rejuvenated Siri at its developer conference in June last year. The company has reportedly been working on a more conversational version of its voice assistant in an effort to compete with OpenAI's ChatGPT and others like it.

The new Siri is reported to utilize more advanced large language models and could potentially allow for back-and-forth conversations and handle requests in a quicker manner.

However, the company's engineers are racing to finish the software, the report said, with some features originally planned for April, potentially being postponed until May or later.

Although Apple (AAPL) announced its AI features with much fanfare in June 2024, it only started rolling them out in December in phases. The company is banking on its much-hyped Apple Intelligence to boost sluggish iPhone sales.

Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) is also reportedly struggling with the Alexa's AI-powered upgrade. The e-commerce giant won't launch a revamped version of its virtual assistant for at least a month after its planned unveiling on February 26, the Washington Post reported.

According to the report, the updated assistant has struggled with giving accurate answers to test questions. An Amazon (AMZN) employee told the outlet that the update won't arrive "until March 31 or later" due to the issues.

Amazon (AMZN) first announced plans to "supercharge" Alexa with AI in September 2023, which said it would take place over a period of months, but which has yet to materialize.

It remains to be seen if Amazon (AMZN) can fulfill founder Jeff Bezos' original vision of the helpful Star Trek "Computer."

More on Apple, Amazon

data.title: 51 of 77 companies deliver EPS wins this week – Earnings Scorecard
data.description:
Wall Street snapped a two-week losing streak as headlines about President Donald Trump's remarks on tariff policy, economic data on inflation and corporate earnings took center stage.

The January U.S. consumer inflation report came in hotter than expected, as both the headline and core consumer price index (CPI) posted their highest M/M readings since March last year. The rise was driven by surging shelter and food prices, with egg prices in particular skyrocketing due to an ongoing outbreak of bird flu.

Besides Trump’s remarks on tariffs, earnings from companies like CVS Health (CVS), McDonald's (MCD), Coca-Cola Company (KO), and Deere (DE) were also in the headlines during the week.

The earnings scorecard for the week was decked in green, with 51 S&P 500 companies reporting EPS wins out of the 77 that reported earnings this week. Out of those, 45 names surpassed revenue estimates.

Among the 14 companies from the healthcare space that reported earnings this week, nine of them beat EPS expectations, while 13 topped revenue estimates.

Shares of CVS Health (CVS) added nearly 15% on Wednesday after the healthcare giant exceeded Q4 Street forecasts for both revenue and earnings, driven by its segments operating its insurance arm, Aetna, and its pharmacy network. Following its results, Cantor Fitzgerald upgraded CVS Health to Overweight from Neutral and increased its 12-month per-share target to $71 from $62.

Other than CVS, healthcare companies like Biogen (BIIB), GE HealthCare Technologies (GHEC), Edwards Lifesciences (EW) topped EPS as well as revenue estimates, while Moderna (MRNA), Humana (HUM) and Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX) reported mixed earnings.

Among the consumer sector companies, seven out of eight beat analysts’ estimates for EPS, excluding McDonald's (MCD), while six companies reported revenue wins.

Burger chain McDonald’s (MCD) was the only consumer company to miss topline as well as bottom-line estimates, as U.S. comparable sales came in at -1.4%, worse than the -0.41% consensus for Q4.

Amid some disappointments by consumer staples stocks in this earnings season, investors cheered Coca-Cola Company’s (KO) earnings after the beverage giant posted a smashing organic sales expectations.

The Industrial sector fared well during the week, with nine out of 12 names beating EPS estimates, two were in-line, while Deere (DE) missed EPS expectations.

The farm equipment maker failed to meet Wall Street EPS expectations and forecasted a significant drop in sales for the year, resulting in shares dropping over 2% on Thursday.

The financial sector had a week of mixed earnings, with nine out of 10 names that reported earnings this week surpassed EPS estimates, while four companies beat revenue estimates.

One of the prominent insurance company, American International Group’s (AIG) solid underwriting performance and top-line growth contributed to robust Q4 earnings. The company reported Q4 non-GAAP EPS of $1.30, beating market expectations by $0.07.

The technology sector fared well revenue wise, as eight out of nine meet market’s revenue estimates. However, four out of nine technology companies missed EPS expectations this week.

Among the prominent names of the sector, Applied Materials (AMAT) and Cisco Systems (CSCO) surpassed topline and bottom-line expectations, while ON Semiconductor (ON) missed EPS and revenue estimates.

Five out of seven real estate companies surpassed EPS estimates this week, while three names beat revenue expectations.

Almost 77% of the S&P 500 companies that have reported results so far this quarter, have recorded an EPS beat, 4% were in-line, while 19% of companies reported a miss. In terms of revenue, 60% of the companies beat on the top line and 40% trailed estimates.

For the upcoming week, 39 companies are scheduled to announce their earnings, including consumer giant Walmart (WMT), energy companies like Occidental Petroleum (OXY) and Devon Energy (DVN), Medtronic (MDT) and mining company Newmont (NEM).

data.title: AppLovin rally fuels valuation debate
data.description:
AppLovin (NASDAQ:APP) blew past Street forecasts with its Q4 2024 financials and full-year outlook this week, sending its shares ~36% higher and sparking a debate among analysts about its sky-high valuation.

After more than a 750% jump over the past 12 months, AppLovin (NASDAQ:APP), which connects advertisers with mobile game developers, trades at a price-to-earnings ratio of 64.1x, implying a ~115% premium to the stock’s five-year average.

However, Seeking Alpha analyst KM Capital cited a 31% overvaluation in the stock after the earnings-led rally, arguing that despite a solid Q4 print, the Palo Alto, California-based company’s balance sheet appears weak in terms of goodwill and long-term debt.

“I am not denying that the company's income statement is flashy at the moment. But flashy does not mean durable, as there are cracks in the foundation,” KM Capital wrote with a Strong Sell recommendation on AppLovin (NASDAQ:APP).

However, SA analyst Juxtaposed Ideas appeared to be on the fence as AppLovin (APP) soared past $500 per share on Friday. “APP's premium valuations are likely to be a double-edged knife if the management is unable to deliver against the consistently raised forward estimates,” Juxtaposed Ideas wrote with a Hold rating.

Meanwhile, considering a longer investment horizon of two years, Deep Value Investing upgraded APP to Strong Buy from Hold, but the SA analyst warned about a potential pullback in the near term.

“A potential announcement in 2025 about the launch of the self-service platform could be a major catalyst for unlocking further growth,” Deep Value Investing wrote, adding, “I believe any potential pullback in the upcoming weeks could be noise considering this timeframe.”

data.title: Notable healthcare headlines for the week: J&J, CVS, Moderna, Biogen in focus
data.description:
Wall Street snapped a two-week losing streak as headlines about President Donald Trump's remarks on tariff policy, economic data on inflation and corporate earnings took center stage.

The S&P 500 Health Care Index Sector (NYSEARCA:XLV) was marginally up during the week.

The top S&P500 healthcare gainers and losers for the last week are as follows:

· Top Gainers: CVS (CVS) +21.52%

· Edwards Lifesciences (EW) +8.34%

· Gilead Sciences (GILD) +8.33%

· GE HealthCare Technologies (GEHC) +5.25%

· Quest Diagnostics (DGX) +3.93%

· Top Losers: West Pharmaceutical Services (WST) -33.20%

· Waters Corporation (WTA) -9.76%

· DaVita (DVA) -9.57%

· Bio-Techne Corporation (TECH) -9.35%

· Mettler-Toledo International (MTD) -8.42%

Here are some of the important healthcare stories from this week:

J&J puts its stroke care business up for sale: Johnson & Johnson (JNJ) has put its stroke care business up for sale, aiming for a valuation of more than $1B, the Financial Times reported on Tuesday, citing people familiar with the matter. "We do not comment on market rumours or speculation," a Johnson & Johnson spokesperson said to Seeking Alpha.

CVS Health jumps as pharmacy and insurance units drive Q4 beat: Shares of CVS Health (CVS) added nearly 15% on Wednesday after the healthcare giant exceeded Q4 Street forecasts for both revenue and earnings, driven by its segments operating its insurance arm, Aetna, and its pharmacy network. Following its results, Cantor Fitzgerald upgraded CVS Health to Overweight from Neutral and increased its 12-month per-share target to $71 from $62.

Moderna swings to Q4 loss after over 65% drop in revenue: Moderna (MRNA) topped the consensus for Q4 revenue but missed Street forecast for EPS, resulting in shares dipping down over 5% in the premarket on Friday.

Biogen outlook disappoints amid pressure on multiple sclerosis franchise: Despite topping Q4 financial results, Shares of Biogen (BIIB) declined over 4% on Wednesday as its full-year outlook disappointed due to its underperforming multiple sclerosis franchise.

Among other news that grabbed headlines this week includes Novartis’ (NVS) acquisition of Anthos Therapeutics for up to $3.1billion, consisting of an upfront payment of $925 million, with potential additional payments of up to $2.15 billion.

Canaccord Genuity upgraded Vertex Pharmaceuticals (VRTX) to Hold from Sell after the cystic fibrosis drugmaker exceeded consensus revenue estimates but missed on earnings with its Q4 2024 financials on Tuesday. The brokerage firm raised its price target to $424 from $408 per share.

Meanwhile, Walgreens Boots Alliance (WBA) suffered a setback in its legal dispute with PWNHealth after a federal court in Delaware ordered it to pay $988M to the telehealth company as an arbitration award for an alleged contract breach.

On the political front, Robert F. Kennedy Jr. will become the next HHS secretary after he attained a majority of Senate votes on his confirmation. The vote was 52-48. All Democrats have voted against his nomination.

Additionally, President Trump is likely to embrace the Medicare drug price negotiations that were initiated under the Biden administration as part of the Inflation Reduction Act, though the president could make some tweaks to put his own spin on it.

data.title: Trending stocks in a mixed week for Wall Street
data.description:
The U.S. stock market had a mixed week amid a flurry of geopolitical and macroeconomic events, including tariff news and new retail sales and inflation data.
The S&P 500 closed out the week 1% higher, but was in red for two of the five sessions. Meanwhile, the tech-heavy Nasdaq Composite was up just over 1.7%, marking its best week so far this year, led by gains made by Big Tech. Both indexes had their third consecutive week in the green.
The larger market was impacted by new tariffs from Trump, who on Monday announced 25% levies on all steel and aluminum imports into the U.S. and later in the week tasked his administration to propose reciprocative tariffs.

On Wednesday, major equity averages had a mixed show, as Wall Street assessed a hotter-than-anticipated monthly U.S. consumer inflation report.

Friday saw new retail data showing a disappointing sales decline for January. Headline retail sales fell more than expected, Bloomberg data showed. The latest producer price index data also showed rising producer prices, suggesting that inflation was picking up again.
Here's what caught our attention this week:
Intel (INTC) was in focus in part due to speculation that the semiconductor giant could enter into some sort of tie up with Taiwan Semiconductor (TSM) for its foundry operations. Shares rallied around 22.5% over the week.
Moderna (MRNA) shares tumbled after the drugmaker recorded another quarterly loss, driven by an unexpected charge of $238M related to the termination of a contract manufacturing agreement during the quarter.
Super Micro Computer (SMCI) was in the spotlight, after the artificial intelligence server company reported preliminary Q2 results and said it expects to have its 2024 annual report filed by Feb. 25, along with its quarterly reports for the Sept. 2024 and Dec. 2024 periods. For period ended Dec 31, the company projects adjusted EPS between $0.58 and $0.60 per share (vs. prior outlook of $0.56 to $0.65 per share) and revenue between $5.6B and $5.7B.
Applied Materials (AMAT) shares fell after the semiconductor equipment maker’s second quarter net sales forecast fell short of the analyst estimates. Analysts note that China restrictions were impacting the company’s sales forecast.
GameStop (GME) shares advanced after media speculations that the video game retailer is weighing a strategy around bitcoin. GameStop is still figuring out if the investment is sensible for the business and may not go through with it, one source told CNBC. The company has $4.6B in cash and cash equivalents as of November 2, 2024, an SEC filing shows.
Alibaba (BABA) was in focus on news that Apple (AAPL) will be working with the Chinese tech giant to bring artificial intelligence features to iPhones in China.
Twilio (TWLO) fell after delivering mixed Q4 results and issuing weaker-than-expected Q1 guidance.
BP (BP) shares rose after reports that activist investor Elliott Management disclosed a nearly 5% stake in the British oil and gas company, worth almost £3.8B (~$4.75B).
British American Tobacco (BAT) fell after the company released a mixed earnings report for 2024, highlighting ongoing challenges. The company reported a full-year pretax profit of £3.54B, vs. previous year's loss of £17.06B, but revenue fell 5.2% to £25.87B.
SoundHound AI (SOUN), Serve Robotics (SERV) and Nano-X Imaging (NNOX) shares fell after Nvidia (NVDA) filed a 13F indicating that the chipmaker exited its stakes in the companies. Meanwhile, WeRide ADRs (WRD) and Nebius Group (NBIS) shares surged after Nvidia revealed positions in the companies. Nvidia also reduced its position in Arm Holdings (ARM) by roughly 44% and owned just over 1.1M shares at the date of the filing.
Steel and aluminum stocks were in focus President Trump signed the executive orders imposing 25% tariffs on all steel and aluminum imports into the U.S. Shares of steel and aluminum producers including Cleveland-Cliffs (CLF), Nucor (NUE), Steel Dynamics (STLD), US Steel (X), Commercial Metals (CMC), Alcoa (AA), Century Aluminum (CENX) and Kaiser Aluminum (KALU) all racked up strong gains in Monday's trading in anticipation of the order.

Airbnb (ABNB) shares rose after the home-rental company reported Q4 results that surpassed expectations and issued an outlook that is seen as positive.

Nvidia (NVDA) +6.8%, SoundHound AI (SOUN) -29.9% and SoFi Technologies (SOFI) +7.8% were among some of the most traded stocks in the U.S. market in recent days.

data.title: Real estate stocks inch up amid lower yields, strong earnings
data.description:
S&P 500 real estate stocks grew marginally this week, as yields continued to go down and the sector posted strong earnings.

The Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (NYSEARCA:XLRE) closed the week 0.21% higher at 42.06, logging gains in three of the five trading sessions.

S&P 500 Equity Real Estate Investment Trusts rose 0.34%, while S&P 500 Real Estate Management & Development was down 1.37%.

The Dow Jones REIT Indx Equity REIT Total Return Index (NASDAQ:REIT:IND) added 0.18%, while the FTSE Nareit All Equity REITs (INDEXFTSE: FNER) was up 0.66%.

The benchmark United States 10-Year Bond Yield shed 2 basis points to close the week at 4.48%.

Treasury yields notably fell on Friday, with an unexpectedly weak reading in January retail sales prompting investors to seek out safety in U.S. bonds.

Meanwhile, Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating system maintained its Hold rating on the ETF.

"XLRE features a poor Risk rating per Seeking Alpha’s quantitative scoring system. High concentration and somewhat volatile price action results in the fund being not exactly a conservative play," said SA author Mike Zaccardi.

"After a few years of increasing Treasury yields, now may be the ideal time to overweight XLRE. At the same time, sentiment in the sector is weak as all the attention is on tech and AI-direct plays," said Zaccardi.

Earnings Scorecard

Seven S&P 500 real estate companies reported earnings this week - CBRE Group (CBRE), Digital Realty Trust (DLR), Equinix (EQIX), Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT), Iron Mountain (IRM), Ventas (VTR), and Welltower (WELL).

Five of the seven exceeded the average analyst estimate in terms of profitability. On the revenue side, three posted a beat, while four trailed consensus.

QTD, 21 S&P 500 real estate companies have delivered their quarterly financial results. About 57% of the companies surpassed earnings consensus, while roughly 48% beat revenue estimates.

Next week, the S&P 500 real estate stocks set to report are CoStar Group (CSGP), Host Hotels & Resorts (HST), and VICI Properties (VICI).

Company News

  • CoStar Group (CSGP) acquired the agricultural data company Ag-Analytics.
  • Scotiabank upgraded Mid-America Apartment Communities (MAA), citing an improved outlook for FFO-per-share growth.
  • Federal REIT (FRT) and Welltower (WELL) maintained their quarterly dividends, while Weyerhaeuser (WY), Ventas (VTR), Equinix (EQIX), and Iron Mountain (IRM) raised the distributions.

Winners & Losers

Health Care REITs were the top gainers among the S&P 500 real estate subsectors.

Senior housing operators Ventas (VTR) and Welltower (WELL) drove most of the subsector gains.

"It has been a solid REIT earnings season in senior housing circles -- both WELL and VTR impressed with the growth outlook for 2025," said Wedbush in a research note, upgrading Welltower to Outperform from Neutral and maintaining its Outperform rating on Ventas.

Iron Mountain (IRM), with a mixed Q4 and soft guidance, was the top loser for the week among the S&P 500 real estate stocks.

Federal Realty Investment Trust (FRT), with Q4 earnings slightly missing the mark, was the next on the list.

Here is a look at the subsector performances for the week:

data.title: Wall Street snaps two-week losing streak, leaving S&P 500 just under record close
data.description:
The S&P 500 (SP500) on Friday advanced 1.47% for the week to end at 6,114.63 points, posting gains in three out of five sessions. Its accompanying SPDR S&P 500 ETF Trust (NYSEARCA:SPY) added 1.49% for the week.

The benchmark index snapped a two-week losing streak, and is now just a whisker away from its record closing high. Wall Street's premier stock average has been on a bit of a roller-coaster over the last two weeks, but after all that still finds itself up nearly 4% for the fledgling year.

Unsurprisingly, another week brought another round of headlines dominated by tariffs. U.S. President Donald Trump on Monday announced 25% tariffs on all steel and aluminum imports into the U.S.

"Imports of these products were close to $108bn in 2024 with tariffs averaging 4.3% on that trade," JPMorgan's Michael Feroli said on Friday. See the table he shared below:

"Consequently the aggregate impact of a 25% tariff on GDP is not that large, though it could clearly be important for those two industries, especially aluminum where imports are a larger share of the market. In both cases, Canada, China, and Mexico are the largest providers, especially Canada in the case of aluminum," Feroli added.

Then, on Thursday, Trump signed a memorandum that tasked several of his federal agencies with investigating current trade relations and proposing reciprocal tariffs on a country-specific basis. Still, the fact that Trump did not immediately put in effect the tit-for-tat tariffs and was scant on details, helped market sentiment.

The other big story of the week was economic data on inflation. The January consumer price index (CPI) report on Wednesday came in significantly hotter than expected, sending markets into a tailspin. Traders immediately moved to price in only one interest rate cut by the Federal Reserve this year.

But the producer price index (PPI) report on Thursday provided some relief. On the surface, it came in towards the hotter side. However, digging deeper, the components in the PPI that directly feed into the core personal consumption expenditures (PCE) price index came in soft for January. Most analysts now see annual growth in the January PCE deflator falling to its lowest reading since early 2021.

Turning to the weekly performance of the S&P 500 (SP500) sectors, nine of the 11 ended in positive territory. The heavyweight Technology sector rose the most, with an advance of nearly 4%. Health Care and Financials were the two losers. See below a breakdown of the performance of the sectors as well as their accompanying SPDR Select Sector ETFs from February 7 close to February 14 close:

#1: Information Technology +3.76%, and the Technology Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLK) +3.07%.

#2: Communication Services +1.99%, and the Communication Services Select Sector SPDR Fund (XLC) +2.50%.

#3: Materials +1.75%, and the Materials Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLB) +1.77%.

#4: Consumer Staples +1.73%, and the Consumer Staples Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLP) +1.59%.

#5: Energy +1.06%, and the Energy Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLE) +1.71%.

#6: Utilities +1.05%, and the Utilities Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLU) +1.23%.

#7: Consumer Discretionary +0.28%, and the Consumer Discretionary Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLY) +0.63%.

#8: Real Estate +0.22%, and the Real Estate Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLRE) +0.21%.

#9: Industrials +0.15%, and the Industrial Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLI) +0.16%.

#10: Financials -0.08%, and the Financial Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLF) -0.04%.

#11: Health Care -1.11%, and the Health Care Select Sector SPDR Fund ETF (XLV) -1.11%.

For investors looking to track the benchmark S&P 500 (SP500), here are some exchange-traded funds of interest: (VOO), (IVV), (RSP), (SSO), (UPRO), (SH), (SDS), and (SPXU).

For investors looking into the future of what's happening, take a look at the Seeking Alpha Catalyst Watch to see next week's breakdown of actionable events that stand out.

data.title: Intel is Hold rated across the board despite Trump upheaval
data.description:
This week, Intel Corporation (NASDAQ:INTC) posted a record rally after Vice President J.D. Vance highlighted the need to manufacture the most advanced AI chips in the U.S. in line with the Trump administration’s America First strategy.

His remarks, closely followed by a Baird report indicating a potential tie-up between the company and its main rival, Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM), sent Intel (NASDAQ:INTC) shares ~24% higher this week, its best weekly performance since 2000.

While the Trump administration has breathed new life into Intel (NASDAQ:INTC), its stock, having lost more than half of its value since December 2023 amid leadership uncertainty and declining sales, remains Hold rated among Wall Street and Seeking Alpha analysts.

Meanwhile, Seeking Alpha's Quant System, which consistently outperforms the market, has indicated an unchanged Hold position on INTC for three consecutive years, according to available data.

The Santa Clara, California-based chip giant lost a quarter of its market capitalization in August after the company, with its Q2 FY24 results, suspended dividends and announced layoffs impacting 15% of its staff amid a $10B cost-reduction plan.

In December, Intel (INTC) announced the abrupt departure of its CEO, Patrick Gelsinger, under whose leadership the company witnessed three consecutive years of sales contractions that led to its first full-year loss in 2024.

Despite Intel's (INTC) recent rally, Citi reaffirmed its Neutral rating on the stock on Thursday, citing multiple reasons why a joint venture between the company and TSMC will not succeed.

“Intel would be beholden to TSMC and different process ecosystems,” analyst Christopher Danely wrote, adding, “We view the success of this type of arrangement as unlikely” and “continue to believe Intel should exit the foundry business.”

This week, SA analyst Bay Area Ideas downgraded Intel (INTC) to Neutral from Buy, citing unfavorable technical indicators in its stock. “As Warren Buffett once stated, a cheap price for a mediocre business may not be a good deal, and I believe Intel is a good example of that currently.”

data.title: Will GameStop keep its retro vibe or pile billions into crypto?
data.description:
GameStop (NYSE:GME) posted a modest gain of 7% last week amid buzz over a possible entry back into the crypto sector. CNBC reported that the retailer is eyeing investments in alternative asset classes, including crypto, and especially bitcoin (BTC-USD).

Sources indicated that GameStop (NYSE:GME) is still figuring out if the investment is sensible for the business. The report caught the attention of investors because GME had $4.6 billion in cash holdings at the end of Q3, compared to its current market cap of $12.1 billion.

Investors may remember that GameStop (GME) has a mixed history with cryptocurrency and blockchain initiatives in general. The company launched a self-custodial cryptocurrency wallet in 2022 that allowed customers to manage digital assets, including cryptocurrencies and non-fungible tokens. The move was part of GameStop's (GME) broader strategy to expand into the digital asset space.

The Texas-based retailer also unveiled an NFT marketplace later in 2022 that was built on the Ethereum-based Loopring Layer 2 network. The company also partnered with Immutable X, an Ethereum-based Layer 2 scaling solution, to integrate its global order book into the GameStop (GME) NFT marketplace.

However, GameStop's (GME) adventures into the crypto world were cut short when the company decided to shut down its cryptocurrency wallet service in 2023. The official reason given at the time for the closure was "regulatory uncertainty" surrounding the crypto industry.

Ryan Cohen has said very little publicly about GameStop's (GME) plans for its cash hoard, which raised the level of intrigue after he posted a photo of him alongside MicroStrategy (MSTR) co-founder and major Bitcoin (BTC-USD) investor Michael Saylor. As for the company itself, most of its marketing and social media interactions this year have highlighted the upside of the physical videogame business, trading cards, and collectibles. The retro and nostalgic appeal to consumers has been unmistakable.

GameStop (GME) is not expected to report Q4 earnings until the middle part of March or later. Options trading implies a share price move of 9% or more before April. Short interest on GME stands at 7.2% of the total float.
